Understanding the Companies, Bottlenecks and Economics Powering the Next Decade of Computing
Understanding where capital flows, bottlenecks emerge and value accrues.
CA PRANAY BHANSALI
Independent research notebook on capital flows, technology infrastructure and value chains.
Field notes, not advice. Nothing here is a recommendation to buy or sell anything. Figures are drawn from public filings, transcripts and trade press as of June 2026, cross-checked where possible and will be wrong in places — that is the nature of writing about a moving system in real time.
FIVE THINGS TO REMEMBER
If you read nothing else in this issue, read this page.
1. A prompt is not one industry. It is six.
Networking, compute, memory, advanced packaging, power and cooling each take a cut. Each has a different owner, a different margin and a different multi-year build cycle. AI exposure as a single idea hides more than it reveals.
2. The current bottleneck is not the interesting one.
Scarcity does not get solved. It moves. GPUs to packaging, packaging to memory, memory to power. The market prices the current location of scarcity well. It prices the next one badly, because the data for the next one does not exist yet.
3. Training and inference are two businesses pretending to be one.
Training is sunk R&D with no direct revenue. Inference is a recurring business with real, improving margins. Most capex commentary adds these together and then asks one question of the sum, which is the wrong question.
4. Falling cost per token and rising capex are not in tension.
If usage per task is growing faster than cost per token is falling, both can be true at once. Most of the AI capex doesn’t make sense commentary misses this arithmetic entirely.
5. Markets are pricing a five year build with a one quarter attention span.
Power plants, grid interconnects and fabs take years. Earnings calls happen every three months. The gap between those two clocks is where most of the mispricing in this sector currently lives.
THE TOOLS USED IN THIS ISSUE
Three lenses recur through this letter and will recur through every future one. None of them are unique to AI. They are general tools for thinking about where capital goes, what it earns and how long that return survives contact with everyone else noticing it too.
Framework 1-The Bottleneck Migration Framework
Scarcity does not disappear when capacity is added. It moves to whatever input has the least elastic supply relative to the layer that was just expanded. The useful question is never what is scarce right now as that is visible to everyone. The useful question is given what just got built, what becomes scarce next. This letter traces that migration through silicon, packaging, memory and power.
Framework 2-The Duration Mismatch Lens
Markets re-rate companies on a quarterly clock. Fabs, power plants and grid interconnects are built on a three to seven year clock. When a company’s near-term stock reaction and its underlying capital commitments are running on clocks of different length, the mismatch between those two durations is often where the opportunity or the risk, actually sits and not in the headline number itself.
Framework 3-The Incremental Dollar Framework
The question is not whether a company is good. The question is where its next dollar of capital goes, what it earns there, whether that return is visible to the market yet and how long it stays that way before competitors close the gap. Applied alongside Framework 1, this becomes sharper: the highest incremental returns tend to sit at the current bottleneck and tend to compress once that bottleneck migrates.
FIELD NOTE
These three tools are not independent. Framework 1 tells you where to look. Framework 3 tells you what you’ll find when you get there. Framework 2 tells you whether the market has already noticed.
Wherever one of these is doing real work in the analysis below, it gets flagged explicitly, the way a margin note would in a working notebook.
You can read the complete report at https://www.linkedin.com/in/ca-pranay-bhansali-474635201/ featured section.
